title: Living in Boulder vlog 3 -  3 - 2017: Bike Racer Levels Pages for world peace

enrichment.summary: Kyle gives a late-night vlog update from Boulder about his ongoing 'levels page' project — a website cataloging bike racing performance tiers across different distances. He explains the data organization system, mentions listening to content about aliens and a small humanoid specimen, and briefly notes he's moving back to Rome in about a month.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: Scientists fail to explain conclusions from beginning to end with actual data; a simpler, more transparent approach is needed
Analyzing endurance sport numbers to understand performance levels is underexplored and valuable
Open curiosity — unsure whether humans descended from aliens or vice versa, sees it as a chicken-or-egg question
Regrets not buying a faster computer sooner; it's enabling him to finally consolidate the project

entities: Stephen (likely Steven Greer or similar alien researcher — unnamed clearly)
Atacama humanoid (implied by description of 6-inch skeleton with large head and near-human DNA)

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I haven't done a video in a few days because I've been gaining momentum on my recently levels page.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I feel like I don't want to do a video unless I feel like I'm making progress on whatever I'm working on.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: For the last few days, I've been gathering my bearings.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I built this bike or a simple page back in 2013 when I moved to Boulder.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I looked at the window and saw the Flatirons and thought I should hike there and be a DRO athlete.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I made a website really badly at first.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I started doing stuff on Google Earth.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I got a new computer, and it's so much faster than my old one.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I feel like I can get a lot done with this new computer.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I have different distance categories for my project: 250 meters, 500 meters, 1 kilometer, 2 kilometers, 4 kilometers, 10 kilometers, 20 kilometers, 40 kilometers, 90 kilometers, 180 kilometers, 360 kilometers, 100 kilometers, and 1,500 kilometers.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I also have a category for a week, which would be around 4,000 kilometers.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I organize these distances by both distance and time.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Within each distance, I try to get international, national, and state-level time trials.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I include non-drafting triathlon data because it's individual.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I throw all the data into levels and organize it by speed.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I plan to do the same with swimming and running, though not to the same extent.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I don't plan to make a downhill hiking category.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I'm collecting data to scientifically assign levels to different categories.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I want to explain how I came to my conclusions logically from beginning to end.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I think there needs to be a new way to practice the scientific method, one that's simpler and more abstract.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Scientists don't do a good job of explaining how they come to their conclusions from beginning to end.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Endurance sports are interesting because you can look at the numbers and figure out how they work.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I don't think people have started to analyze endurance sports data in this way.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I've completed the 10-kilometer and 200-meter categories for my levels page.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I'm now working on the 140-kilometer category.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: My old computer was slow, and I regret not getting a faster one sooner.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I'm reorganizing my data to ensure all time trials make it to the levels page.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I'm color-coding and numbering the data, which will amount to a couple thousand entries.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I plan to put the data up on the circus next month.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The circus is 40 lanes wide, and I might have people tail outside of it if the race or levels don't fit.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The fastest speed in my data is 33 miles per hour, and the slowest is 20 miles per hour.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Each lap is almost a kilometer, and 40 laps would be 40 kilometers.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: This project will take a while to complete.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I've been listening to random stuff over the last week, including weed and aliens.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I don't know what weed is because I have a habit of tearing off the answers and throwing them away without thinking.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I've been listening to Stephen Greer talk about the Salaline alien, which is six inches tall with a big head.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The Salaline alien has 99% similar DNA to humans, with 10% extra DNA we don't understand.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: It's a chicken-or-egg question whether we came from aliens or aliens came from us.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The same question applies to our alphabet—whether it came from aliens or we developed it independently.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I'm going to sleep now and will check in later.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I'm still organizing and figuring out what I have left to do.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I'll get an idea of how much longer it will take to finish this project.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I'm about a month away from bouncing back to Rome.

transcript.text: Hello, Living in Boulder V-Log at 7-11 p.m. on March 3rd, 2018.

I can still tell the difference between this computer and that other computer.
This computer is so much faster, I can do so much.
So yeah, I feel like I'm getting a lot done.
And so I'll explain what I'm doing with it.
So I have 12 different distance categories.

2 kilometers to 4 kilometers.
10 kilometers-ish.

Because that's individual too.
The non-drafting triathlon.
So, and then I get, and then I throw them all in the level.
And in certain distances, you know, when it's time trials from different distances, I just organize it.
Because I throw them all together, I organize it by their speed.
And then I put them on the Circus Maximus in Rome.
And I'll probably end up doing that with swimming and running too.
Not to the same extent though.
But, um, and I'll make a downhill biking one.
Um, but, uh, yeah, so, and then I'm going to get and, um, collect exactly how many people from each category are in each of the different levels.
And put a number to it.
And then that way I can assign, like scientifically assign, exactly what level different categories are.

Um, I think it's, uh, because then I can explain how I came to the, my conclusion, you know, from like organizing all categories and how much training it takes to get these different levels and categories.
Uh, but I can explain from the beginning to the end.
Because I can explain how I did it.

If you want to do that enough, it just kind of seems like you have to, there has to be a new way to look at science.
